Cleveland Browns:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Cleveland National Air Show (43805784984).jpg|thumb|300x300px|The Browns have played their home games at Cleveland Browns Stadium since 1999.]] The [[Cleveland Browns]] are a professional [[American football]] franchise based in [[Cleveland, Ohio|Cleveland]], [[Ohio]]. They are a member of the [[AFC North|North Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The team began playing in 1946 as a charter member of the [[All-America Football Conference]] (AAFC), and joined the NFL as part of the [[1950 NFL season#The AAFC–NFL merger|AAFC–NFL merger]] in 1950.{{cite web |url=http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/team/history/results.php |title=Year By Year Season Results |publisher=NFL Enterprises LLC |work=[[Cleveland Browns]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100402101044/http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/team/history/results.php |archive-date=April 2, 2010 |url-status=dead |access-date=January 22, 2013}} The team played their home games at [[Cleveland Stadium]] from 1946 to 1995 before moving to [[Cleveland Browns Stadium]], where they have played since 1999.{{Cite web |url=http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/tickets/stadium/ |title=Cleveland Browns Stadium |publisher=NFL Enterprises LLC |work=Cleveland Browns |access-date=January 22, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100315201301/http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/tickets/stadium/ |archive-date=March 15, 2010 |url-status=dead }} The Browns did not play from 1996 to 1998 when the team's owner, [[Art Modell]], moved the team to [[Baltimore, Maryland|Baltimore]], [[Maryland]] and formed the [[Baltimore Ravens]]. The team was reactivated under new ownership in Cleveland in 1999.{{harvnb|Henkel|2005|p=111}} The Browns are currently owned by [[Jimmy Haslam III]].{{cite web |url=http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/team/front-office.html |date=January 20, 2013 |title=Cleveland Browns – Front Office |access-date=January 22, 2013 |archive-date=January 23, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130123205511/http://www.clevelandbrowns.com/team/front-office.html |url-status=live }} There have been 19 non-interim [[head coach]]es for the Browns franchise. Their first head coach was [[Paul Brown]], who coached for 17 complete seasons. Brown is also the franchise's all-time leader for the most regular season games coached (214), the most regular season game wins (158), the most playoffs games coached (14), and the most playoff game wins (9). Brown is the only Browns head coach to win an AAFC championship, winning four. Brown and [[Blanton Collier]] have led the Browns to NFL championships. Brown won the ''[[Sporting News]]'' [[National Football League Coach of the Year Award|NFL Coach of the Year]] three times, the [[United Press International]] (UPI) NFL Coach of the Year once,{{cite web|url=http://www.hickoksports.com/history/nflcoy.shtml |title=NFL Coach of the Year Award |last=Hickok |first=Ralph |date=April 17, 2008 |publisher=Hick OK Sports |url-status=dead |access-date=January 22, 2013 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20121205094433/http://www.hickoksports.com/history/nflcoy.shtml |archive-date=December 5, 2012 }} and was elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] as a coach. Five coaches have won Coach of the Year awards: Brown ([[1949 NFL season|1949]], [[1951 NFL season|1951]], [[1953 NFL season|1953]], [[1957 NFL season|1957]]), [[Forrest Gregg]] ([[1976 NFL season|1976]]), [[Sam Rutigliano]] ([[1979 NFL season|1979]], [[1980 NFL season|1980]]), [[Marty Schottenheimer]] ([[1986 NFL season|1986]]), and [[Kevin Stefanski]] ([[2020 NFL season|2020]], [[2023 NFL season|2023]]). Since their reactivation in 1999, the Browns have had significant turnover at the head coaching position. Eleven men have coached the Browns since 1999, and only two have reached the postseason: [[Butch Davis]] ([[2002 Cleveland Browns season|2002]]) and Stefanski ([[2020 Cleveland Browns season|2020]] and [[2023 Cleveland Browns season|2023]]). ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Browns. {{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Tony Dungy Navy.jpg|200px|thumb|upright|[[Tony Dungy]] is a former head coach of the Colts. He led them to their second Super Bowl victory in Miami ([[Super Bowl XLI|XLI]]).]] The [[Indianapolis Colts]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Indianapolis]], [[Indiana]]. They are a member of the [[American Football Conference South Division|South Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). In 1953, a [[Baltimore, Maryland|Baltimore]]-based group led by [[Carroll Rosenbloom]] won the rights to a new Baltimore franchise. Rosenbloom was granted an NFL team, and was awarded the holdings of the defunct [[Dallas Texans (NFL)|Dallas Texans]] organization. The team was known as the Baltimore Colts for 31 seasons before moving to [[Indianapolis, Indiana|Indianapolis]] in March 1984.{{cite web |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=14 |title=Indianapolis Colts |access-date=June 18, 2008 |publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame |archive-date=June 12, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080612154940/http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=14 |url-status=live }} There have been 19{{efn|Although [[Bruce Arians]] was named as interim head coach during the 2012 season, he was not credited with the team's record while in charge.}} head coaches for the Colts franchise. [[Keith Molesworth]] became the first coach of the Baltimore Colts in 1953, but he was reassigned to a different position with the team following the season.{{cite web |url=http://www.sportsecyclopedia.com/nfl/balticolts/baltcolts.html |title=Baltimore Colts: Historical Moments |access-date=June 18, 2008 |publisher=Baltimore Colts |archive-date=February 10, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070210173055/http://www.sportsecyclopedia.com/nfl/balticolts/baltcolts.html |url-status=live }} In terms of tenure, [[Weeb Ewbank]] has led the team for more games (112) and more complete seasons (nine) than any other head coach. He led the team to two of their [[List of NFL champions|NFL championships]]. Four Colts head coaches; Ewbank, [[Don Shula]] (3), [[Ted Marchibroda]], and [[Tony Dungy]] have been named [[National Football League Coach of the Year Award|coach of the year]] by at least one major news organization. Ewbank, Shula, and Dungy are members of the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]], having been inducted in 1978, 1997, and 2016 respectively.{{cite news |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/coach-ewbank-laid-to-rest/ |title=Coach Ewbank Laid To Rest |access-date=June 19, 2008 |publisher=CBS News |date=November 18, 1998 |archive-date=May 24, 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110524133811/http://www.cbsnews.com/stories/1998/11/18/archive/main22907.shtml |url-status=live }}{{cite web|url=http://www.nationwidespeakers.com/speaker/89/don-shula/nfl-coaching-legend |title=Don Shula: NFL Coaching Legend |access-date=June 19, 2008 |publisher=Nationwide |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080517043518/http://www.nationwidespeakers.com/speaker/89/don-shula/nfl-coaching-legend |archive-date=May 17, 2008 }} Six times in Colts history there were interim head coaches. In 1972, [[Don McCafferty]] was fired five games into the season. [[John Sandusky]] was named as the interim head coach for the rest of the season, during which he led the Colts to a 4–5 record, but he was not made the permanent coach the next year. In 1974, head coach [[Howard Schnellenberger]] started off the season 0–3 and was fired. [[Joe Thomas (football coach)|Joe Thomas]] assumed the duties of head coach and finished the season at 2–12. In 1991, the Colts started off 0–5 and [[Ron Meyer]] was fired as head coach. [[Rick Venturi]] was named as the interim for the final 11 games.{{cite web |url=https://www.sportsecyclopedia.com/nfl/indy/icolts.html |title=Indianapolis Colts: Historical Moments |access-date=June 19, 2008 |publisher=Indianapolis Colts |archive-date=September 9, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230909062525/https://sportsecyclopedia.com/nfl/indy/icolts.html |url-status=live }} In 2005 [[Tony Dungy]] was forced to miss one game due to [[2005 Indianapolis Colts season#Tony Dungy and family experience tragedy|personal issues]]. [[Jim Caldwell (American football)|Jim Caldwell]] was named as the one game interim.{{cite web |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/news/story?id=2268593 |title=Dungy's son, 18, found dead in Tampa suburb |date=December 22, 2005 |access-date=June 18, 2008 |publisher=ESPN |archive-date=May 22, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080522084634/http://sports.espn.go.com/nfl/news/story?id=2268593 |url-status=live }} In 2012 offensive coordinator [[Bruce Arians]] was named as the interim head coach indefinitely after [[Chuck Pagano]] was diagnosed with [[leukemia]] four weeks into the 2012 NFL season.{{cite web |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/8447831/indianapolis-colts-coach-chuck-pagano-leukemia |title=Chuck Pagano battling leukemia |date=October 2012 |access-date=October 1, 2012 |publisher=ESPN |archive-date=October 3, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121003005429/http://espn.go.com/nfl/story/_/id/8447831/indianapolis-colts-coach-chuck-pagano-leukemia |url-status=live }} Arians led the Colts to a 9–3 record – the record being credited to Pagano – and made the playoffs. Following a 3–5–1 start, Frank Reich was fired and on November 7, 2022, the Indianapolis Colts named Jeff Saturday as the team's interim coach for the rest of the season, during which he led the Colts to a 1–7 record. Washington Commanders:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Dan Quinn training camp 2025.jpg|thumb|right|[[Dan Quinn (American football)|Dan Quinn]], Washington's current head coach, was hired in 2024.]] The [[Washington Commanders]] are a professional [[American football]] franchise based in the [[Washington metropolitan area]]. They are members of the [[NFC East|East]] division in the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC) of the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The Commanders were founded in {{nfly|1932}} as the [[Boston]] Braves, named after the [[History of the Boston Braves|local baseball franchise]].{{cite web|title=Washington Commanders Team History|url=https://www.profootballhof.com/teams/washington-commanders/team-history/|website=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]|access-date=March 14, 2024|language=en}} The franchise changed its name the following year to the Redskins and moved to [[Washington, D.C.]] in {{nfly|1937}}. In {{nfly|2020}}, the team retired the Redskins name after [[Washington Redskins name controversy|longstanding controversies]] surrounding it and briefly played as the Washington Football Team before becoming the Commanders in {{nfly|2022}}. There have been 31 head coaches for the franchise, four of which played for the franchise during their career. [[Joe Gibbs]] is the only coach to have more than one tenure with the team.{{Cite web |title=Joe Gibbs' Coaching Record|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/GibbJo0.htm|access-date=March 26, 2024|website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]]|language=en}} [[Ray Flaherty]] coached teams that won the [[1937 NFL Championship Game|1937]] and [[1942 NFL Championship]]s, while Joe Gibbs coached teams that won [[Super Bowl XVII]] (1982), [[Super Bowl XXII]] (1987), and [[Super Bowl XXVI]] (1991).{{cite web|work=NFLTeamHistory.com |url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/washington_redskins/championship_history.html |title=Washington Redskins Championship History |access-date=March 14, 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080422032749/http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/washington_redskins/championship_history.html |archive-date=April 22, 2008 }} Gibbs is the franchise leader in games coached and wins, with [[Dudley DeGroot]] leading in [[winning percentage]] with .737.{{Cite web |title=Dudley DeGroot's Coaching Record|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/DeGrDu0.htm|access-date=March 26, 2024|website=[[Pro-Football-Reference.com]]|language=en}} Seven are [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] members: Flaherty, [[Turk Edwards]], [[Curly Lambeau]], [[Otto Graham]], [[Vince Lombardi]], [[George Allen (American football coach)|George Allen]], and Gibbs.{{cite web | title=Hall of Famers by Franchise | work=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] | url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx | access-date=March 14, 2008 }} ==Coaches== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coaches{{efn|A running total of the number of coaches of the Redskins. Dallas Cowboys:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Tom Landry Jan 1997.jpg|200px|thumb|right|Tom Landry was the first head coach of the Cowboys, and coached the team from 1960 to 1988. He was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 1990.]] The [[Dallas Cowboys]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Frisco, Texas]]. Their stadium is located in [[Arlington, Texas]]. They are members of the [[NFC East|Eastern Division]] of the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The Cowboys franchise was founded in 1960 as an expansion team.{{cite web | work=[[Dallas Cowboys]] | url=http://www.dallascowboys.com/history_year.cfm | title=1950 Dallas Cowboys | access-date=March 15, 2008 |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20080308062403/http://www.dallascowboys.com/history_year.cfm |archive-date = March 8, 2008}} The team played their games in the [[Cotton Bowl (stadium)|Cotton Bowl]] from 1960 to 1970, then in [[Texas Stadium]] from 1971 to 2008, and [[AT&T Stadium]] from 2009 to present. There have been nine [[head coach]]es for the Dallas Cowboys. Three coaches have won Super Bowls with the team: [[Tom Landry]] in [[Super Bowl VI]] and [[Super Bowl XII|XII]], [[Jimmy Johnson (American football coach)|Jimmy Johnson]] in [[Super Bowl XXVII]] and [[Super Bowl XXVIII|XXVIII]], and [[Barry Switzer]] in [[Super Bowl XXX]].{{cite web | work=NFLTeamHistory.com | url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/dallas_cowboys/championship_history.html | title=Dallas Cowboys Championship History | access-date=March 15, 2008}} Landry is the team's all-time leader in games coached and wins, and Switzer leads all coaches in regular season [[winning percentage]] with .625. [[Dave Campo]] is the only Cowboys coach with a losing record (.313), and is also the only coach in franchise history to have never posted a winning season. The team's first coach, Tom Landry, has been inducted into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]].{{cite web | title=Hall of Famers by Franchise | work=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] | url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx | access-date=March 15, 2008 }} The current coach is [[Brian Schottenheimer]], who replaced [[Mike McCarthy]] on January 24, 2025, after the latter's contract expired on January 13. ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of coaches of the Cowboys. Philadelphia Eagles:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} This is a '''list of head coaches for the Philadelphia Eagles'''. The [[Philadelphia Eagles]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[Philadelphia]], [[Pennsylvania]]. The Eagles joined the [[National Football League]] (NFL) as an [[expansion team]] in 1933. Currently members of the [[NFC East|Eastern Division]] of the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC), the team has won five [[List of NFL champions|NFL titles]] and made five [[Super Bowl]] appearances ([[Super Bowl XV|1980]], [[Super Bowl XXXIX|2004]], [[Super Bowl LII|2017]], [[Super Bowl LVII|2022]], and [[Super Bowl LIX|2024]]), with their first Super Bowl victory coming in [[Super Bowl LII]] under [[Doug Pederson]] and their second coming in [[Super Bowl LIX]] under [[Nick Sirianni]]. Four coaches have won [[History of National Football League Championship|NFL championships]] with the team: [[Greasy Neale|Earl "Greasy" Neale]] in [[1948 NFL Championship Game|1948]] and [[1949 NFL Championship Game|1949]], [[Buck Shaw]] in [[1960 NFL Championship Game|1960]], Doug Pederson in [[Super Bowl LIX|2017]], and Nick Sirianni in [[Super Bowl LIX|2024]].{{cite web |publisher=NFLTeamHistory.com |url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/philadelphia_eagles/championship_history.html |title=Philadelphia Eagles Championship History |access-date=March 15, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080517082645/http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/philadelphia_eagles/championship_history.html |archive-date=May 17, 2008 |url-status=dead }} [[Andy Reid]] is the all-time leader in games coached and wins,{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/ReidAn0.htm|title=Andy Reid's Coaching Record|publisher=Pro Football Reference|access-date=March 16, 2008|archive-date=September 18, 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100918113035/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/ReidAn0.htm|url-status=live}} while Sirianni has the highest winning percentage with .676 (with at least one full season coached). [[Bert Bell]] is statistically the worst coach the Eagles have had in terms of winning percentage, with a .185 win/loss percentage.{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/BellBe0.htm |title=Bert Bell's Coaching Record |publisher=Pro Football Reference |access-date=March 16, 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080303235621/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/BellBe0.htm |archive-date=March 3, 2008 }} Of the 24 Eagles coaches, five have been elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]].{{cite web |title=Hall of Famers by Franchise |publisher=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] |url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx |access-date=March 14, 2008 |archive-date=October 15, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151015213446/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx |url-status=live }} [[Bert Bell]] was a charter member of the Hall of Fame. Bell was inducted for his work as the NFL Commissioner from 1946 to 1959. [[Wayne Millner]], who coached the team in 1951, was enshrined as a player in 1968. [[Greasy Neale]] was in the class of 1969 for his work as the Eagles coach in the 1940s. [[Mike McCormack (American football)|Mike McCormack]] made the 1984 class for his Offensive Tackle play. [[Dick Vermeil]] entered the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] in 2022 for his coaching career with the Eagles, Rams and Chiefs. Several former NFL players have been head coaches for the Eagles, including [[Jerry Williams (American football)|Jerry Williams]], [[Ed Khayat]], and [[Marion Campbell]]. Andy Reid{{cite magazine |magazine=[[Sports Illustrated]] |url=http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/football/nfl/1998/playoffs/news/1999/01/11/pk_mmqb/ |title=The Racial Scorecard |access-date=March 15, 2008 |date=January 11, 1999 |archive-date=December 18, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071218083016/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/football/nfl/1998/playoffs/news/1999/01/11/pk_mmqb/ |url-status=dead }} spent 14 seasons in charge before he was fired on December 31, 2012, after a 4–12 season – Reid's worst season in charge – which left the Eagles bottom of the NFC.{{cite news|url=http://www.philadelphiaeagles.com/news/article-1/Eagles-Announce-End-Of-Reids-Tenure/02b4c730-d341-4dd5-aa03-fecd9728c4ba|title=Eagles Announce End Of Reid's Tenure|work=[[Philadelphia Eagles]]|publisher=NFL Enterprises LLC|date=December 31, 2012|access-date=December 31, 2012|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130102114214/http://www.philadelphiaeagles.com/news/article-1/Eagles-Announce-End-Of-Reids-Tenure/02b4c730-d341-4dd5-aa03-fecd9728c4ba|archive-date=January 2, 2013}}{{cite magazine |magazine=[[Sports Illustrated]] |url=http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/football/nfl/1998/playoffs/news/1999/01/11/pk_mmqb/ |title=The Racial Scorecard |access-date=March 15, 2008 |date=January 11, 1999 |archive-date=December 18, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071218083016/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/football/nfl/1998/playoffs/news/1999/01/11/pk_mmqb/ |url-status=dead }} He was replaced by former [[Oregon Ducks football|University of Oregon]] head coach [[Chip Kelly]], who led the Eagles to a 10–6 record and the playoffs. Kelly was fired on December 29, 2015, after going 6–9 through that season's first 15 games. He was replaced by offensive coordinator [[Pat Shurmur]] for Week 17. [[Doug Pederson]] served five seasons with the Eagles as their head coach before being fired on January 11, 2021, after leading the team to a 4–11–1 record in his final year as head coach. [[Nick Sirianni]] has been head coach since January 24, 2021.{{cite web | last=Spadaro | first=Dave | title=Eagles announce Nick Sirianni as head coach | website=Philadelphia Eagles | date=2021-01-24 | url=https://www.philadelphiaeagles.com/news/eagles-announce-nick-sirianni-as-head-coach | access-date=2026-04-24}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:90%;" |- |'''#''' |Number of coaches{{refn|A running total of the number of coaches of the Eagles. New York Giants:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=May 2021}} {{Use American English|date=September 2022}} The [[New York Giants]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in [[East Rutherford, New Jersey]]. They are members of the [[NFC East|East Division]] of the [[National Football Conference]] (NFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The franchise was founded in {{nfly|1925}} and have played for 19 [[History of National Football League Championship|NFL championships]]. They have won seven World Championship Games ([[Super Bowl]] and NFL Championship games) and one NFL Championship by virtue of having the league's best record at the end of the season in 1927.{{cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2005/10/25/sports/football/25cnd-mara.html |title=Wellington Mara, Co-Owner of New York Giants, Is Dead at 89 |access-date=2008-04-04 |last=Goldstein |first=Richard |date=2005-10-25 |work=New York Times |archive-date=March 11, 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120311033645/http://www.nytimes.com/2005/10/25/sports/football/25cnd-mara.html |url-status=live }}{{cite web | work=NFLTeamHistory.com | url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/new_york_giants/championship_history.html | title=New York Giants Championship History | access-date=2008-03-16 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080517082901/http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/new_york_giants/championship_history.html | archive-date=2008-05-17 | url-status=dead }} There have been 22 head coaches for the Giants franchise. Five coaches have won [[NFL Championships]] with the team: [[Earl Potteiger]] in {{nfly|1927}}, [[Steve Owen (American football)|Steve Owen]] in [[1934 NFL Championship Game|1934]] and [[1938 NFL Championship Game|1938]], [[Jim Lee Howell]] in [[1956 NFL Championship Game|1956]], [[Bill Parcells]] in [[Super Bowl XXI|1986]] and [[Super Bowl XXV|1990]], and [[Tom Coughlin]] in [[Super Bowl XLII|2007]] and [[Super Bowl XLVI|2011]]. Steve Owen leads all-time in games coached and wins, and [[LeRoy Andrews]] leads all coaches in winning percentage with .828 (with at least one full season coached).{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/AndrRo0.htm|title=LeRoy Andrews' Coaching Record|publisher=Pro Football Reference|access-date=2008-03-17|archive-date=February 19, 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080219180034/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/AndrRo0.htm|url-status=live}} [[Bill Arnsparger]] is statistically the worst coach the Giants have had in terms of winning percentage, with .200.{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/ArnsBi0.htm|title=Bill Arnsparger's Coaching Record|publisher=Pro Football Reference|access-date=2008-03-17|archive-date=December 22, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171222051428/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/ArnsBi0.htm|url-status=live}} Of the 22 Giants coaches, three have been elected into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]]: [[Benny Friedman]], Steve Owen and Bill Parcells.{{cite web | title=Hall of Famers by Franchise | work=[[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] | url=http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx | access-date=March 16, 2008 | archive-date=October 15, 2015 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151015213446/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/teams.aspx | url-status=live }} Several former players have been head coach for the Giants, including [[Doc Alexander]], Earl Potteiger, Benny Friedman, Steve Owen, [[Jim Lee Howell]], and [[Alex Webster (gridiron football)|Alex Webster]]. The current head coach of the Giants is [[John Harbaugh]], since his hiring on January 17, 2026.{{Cite web|url=https://www.giants.com/news/john-harbaugh-hired-head-coach-new-york-giants-nfl|title=New York Giants hire John Harbaugh as head coach|first=Dan|last=Salomone|publisher=[[New York Giants]]|date=January 19, 2026|accessdate=January 19, 2026}} ==Key== {| class="wikitable" |- |'''#''' |Number of coachesA running total of the number of Giants head coaches. Jacksonville Jaguars:
{{Short description|none}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[File:Army.Coughlin.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Tom Coughlin, the Jaguars' first coach from {{nfly|1995}} to {{nfly|2002}}, is also the most successful coach in Jaguars team history, as measured by playoff appearances, conference championship appearances, and overall winning percentage of games won.]] The [[Jacksonville Jaguars]] are a professional [[American football]] franchise based in [[Jacksonville, Florida]]. They are members of the [[AFC South|South Division]] of the [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) in the [[National Football League]] (NFL). The team, along with the [[Carolina Panthers]], joined the NFL as [[expansion team]]s in 1995. The Jaguars, along with the [[Houston Texans]], have never played in a Super Bowl or any other NFL Championship, but have made 3 appearances in AFC Championship games; twice against the New England Patriots after the 1996 and the 2017 seasons, and once against the Tennessee Titans after the 1999 season. The Jaguars have had nine head coaches since their inaugural [[1995 Jacksonville Jaguars season|1995 season]], including two interim coaches. [[Tom Coughlin]] and [[Jack Del Rio]] each won 68 games while coaching the Jaguars, though Coughlin is more successful in terms of [[winning percentage]], winning 53.1% of his games in charge. Their current head coach is [[Liam Coen]], who was hired on January 24, 2025.{{Cite web|url=https://www.jaguars.com/news/k000151-jacksonville-jaguars-agree-to-terms-with-liam-coen-to-become-head-coach|title=Jacksonville Jaguars Agree to Terms with Liam Coen to Become Head Coach|publisher=[[Jacksonville Jaguars]]|date=January 24, 2025|access-date=January 24, 2025}} ==History== ===Tom Coughlin era (1995–2002)=== The Jaguars first head coach was [[Tom Coughlin]], who was hired by owner [[Wayne Weaver]] well over a year before the team's inaugural season in 1995. The Jaguars won four games their first season, surpassing the previous record of wins for an expansion team (three), but falling well short of the [[Carolina Panthers]] record-setting pace that same year of seven wins. The next year, 1996, Coughlin and the Jaguars went to the AFC Championship game, a feat repeated in 1999, but both years the Jaguars fell short of the Super Bowl. In 2002, Coughlin was fired. ===Jack Del Rio era (2003–2011)=== [[File:Jack del Rio 2008.jpg|thumb|100px|left|Jack Del Rio is the longest-serving head coach of the Jaguars, coaching the franchise between {{nfly|2003}} and {{nfly|2011}}]]Hired to replace Coughlin was [[Jack Del Rio]]. He was hired on January 16, 2003{{cite web |last=Mortensen |first=Chris |author-link=Chris Mortensen |last2=Pasquarelli |first2=Len |author-link2=Len Pasquarelli |date=January 16, 2003 |title=Coordinator Del Rio replaces Coughlin in Jax |url=https://www.espn.com/espn/print?id=1493632&type=news |access-date=March 18, 2008 |publisher=ESPN}} and fired on November 29, 2011. He was replaced on an interim basis with [[Mel Tucker]].{{cite news|work=NFL.com|publisher=[[National Football League]]|url=https://www.nfl.com/news/jaguars-fire-coach-del-rio-after-eight-plus-seasons-09000d5d82493c0b|title=Jaguars fire coach Del Rio after eight-plus seasons|date=November 29, 2011|access-date=November 29, 2011}} Del Rio coached the team from 2003 to 2011, recording a winning percentage of 48.9% from 139 regular season games, but was fired after going over eight full seasons without winning a division title. ===Mike Mularkey era (2012)=== In 2012, [[Mike Mularkey]] was hired as head coach. His team was hit by several key injuries throughout the season and managed going on 2–14, the worst record in franchise history at the time. As a result, new owner Shahid Khan decided he wanted new leadership and fired the General Manager (GM), Gene Smith. The new GM, Dave Caldwell, decided to fire Mike Mularkey. ===Gus Bradley era (2013–2016)=== In [[2013 Jacksonville Jaguars season|2013]], the Jaguars hired [[Gus Bradley]] to become their new head coach. Bradley served a total of four years as the head coach of the Jaguars ({{nfly|2013}}–{{nfly|2016}}), but never was able to make the playoffs. Bradley was fired shortly before the end of the [[2016 Jacksonville Jaguars season|2016 season]], holding a record of 14–48. ===Doug Marrone era (2016–2020)=== [[Doug Marrone]] replaced Bradley for the final two remaining games of the 2016 season as the interim head coach of the Jaguars. On January 9, 2017, the Jaguars removed the interim tag and hired Marrone to be their fifth head coach. In Marrone's first season as head coach, the Jaguars finished with a 10–6 regular season record in [[2017 Jacksonville Jaguars season|2017]], made the playoffs for the first time since [[2007 Jacksonville Jaguars season|2007]] and made an [[2017-18 NFL playoffs|AFC Conference Championship]] appearance but were defeated by the [[2017 New England Patriots season|New England Patriots]]. Over the next three seasons under Marrone, the Jaguars finished 4th in the [[AFC South]] with losing seasons and failed to make the playoffs for three consecutive seasons. In [[2020 Jacksonville Jaguars season|2020]], Marrone led the Jaguars to a franchise-worst and league worst 1–15 season record in 2020, clinching the Jaguars their first franchise [[List of first overall National Football League draft picks|1st overall pick]] in the [[2021 NFL draft]]. Following the end of the 2020 season, Marrone was fired on January 4, 2021. ===Urban Meyer year (2021)=== On January 14, 2021, [[Urban Meyer]] was hired as the sixth head coach of the Jaguars, replacing [[Doug Marrone]]. On February 10, 2021, Meyer hired [[Chris Doyle (American football)|Chris Doyle]] out of [[University of Iowa]] to become the Jaguars director of sports performance. He was accused of racially motivated actions against Iowa players, which eventually led to a subpoena of both Meyer and then-GM [[Trent Baalke]]. Doyle resigned 2 days later. On September 12, 2021, Meyer began his NFL coaching debut with the Jaguars losing to the [[2021 Houston Texans season|Houston Texans]] by a score of 21–37. On October 1, 2021, Meyer was publicly reprimanded by Jaguars owner [[Shad Khan]] for his behavior seen on social media videos that occurred at his Columbus-area restaurant, Urban Meyer's Pint House. Meyer was seen on video in a bar with a young woman who is not his wife, and appeared to grope the woman. The events took place a day after a loss to the [[Cincinnati Bengals]], dropping the Jaguars' record to 0–4 on the season. He apologized to the team and personnel.{{cite web | url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/32342897/jacksonville-jaguars-owner-shad-khan-urban-meyer-regain-our-trust-respect-inexcusable-conduct | title=Jacksonville Jaguars owner Shad Khan: Urban Meyer 'must regain our trust and respect' after 'inexcusable' conduct | website=ESPN.com | last=DiRocco | first=Michael | date=October 5, 2021 | access-date=October 5, 2021 | language=en}} On October 17, 2021, the Jaguars won against the [[2021 Miami Dolphins season|Miami Dolphins]] by a score of 23–20, earning Meyer his 1st career win as an NFL head coach. The win snapped the Jaguars' 20-game losing streak that dated back to September 13, 2020 – week 1 of the [[2020 NFL season]]. On November 7, 2021, Meyer and the Jaguars defeated the heavily favored [[2021 Buffalo Bills season|Buffalo Bills]] by a score of 9–6. Toward the end of the 2021 season, reports surfaced of Jaguars players and coaches being critical of Meyer's treatment of them, to which the team responded with assertions of his job security.{{Cite web|title=Report: Jaguars players, coaches fed up with Urban Meyer on and off field|url=https://sports.yahoo.com/nfl-jaguars-players-coaches-fed-up-with-urban-meyer-on-and-off-field-173919802.html|access-date=December 12, 2021|website=sports.yahoo.com|language=en-US}}{{Cite web|date=December 11, 2021|title=Sources: Jags plan to keep Urban amid tension|url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/32845194/urban-meyer-jacksonville-jaguars-plans-building-tension-sources-say|access-date=December 12, 2021|website=ESPN.com|language=en}} On December 12, 2021, Meyer lost 20–0 to the [[Tennessee Titans]], giving the Jaguars their 1st losing shutout since 2009 at the time. On December 15, 2021, Meyer was accused of kicking former kicker [[Josh Lambo]] during a pregame warmup on August 19, 2021, against the [[Dallas Cowboys]]. On December 16, 2021, Meyer was fired by the Jaguars and the offensive coordinator, [[Darrell Bevell]], was named Meyer's replacement as head coach on an interim basis.{{Cite web|title=A Statement from Shad Khan|url=https://www.jaguars.com/news/a-statement-from-shad-khan|website=Jaguars.com|access-date=December 16, 2021|archive-date=December 16, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211216055506/https://www.jaguars.com/news/a-statement-from-shad-khan|url-status=dead}} Meyer finished his tenure in Jacksonville with a 2–11 (.154) Las Vegas Raiders:
{{Short description|None}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2024}} [[Image:John Madden in 2007.jpg|thumb|200px|right|[[John Madden]] was the head coach of the Oakland Raiders for ten seasons, from 1969 to 1978.]] The [[Las Vegas Raiders]] are a professional [[American football]] team based in the [[Las Vegas Valley]]. The Raiders compete in the [[National Football League]] (NFL) as a member club of the league's [[American Football Conference]] (AFC) [[AFC West|West division]]. Their stadium is located in [[Paradise, Nevada]]. There have been 25 head coaches in Oakland, Los Angeles, and Las Vegas franchise history. Their current coach is [[Klint Kubiak]], who replaced [[Pete Carroll]] after he coached the team for one season. The Raiders franchise was founded in [[Oakland, California]] in 1959 and became the eighth member of the [[American Football League]] (AFL) in 1960 as a replacement for the [[Minnesota Vikings]], who had moved to the NFL. The Raiders joined the NFL in 1970, after the [[AFL–NFL merger]]. They played in [[Los Angeles]] between 1982 and 1995, before returning to Oakland.{{cite web|url=http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=23|title=Oakland Raiders|publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame|access-date=April 15, 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061230070530/http://www.profootballhof.com/history/team.jsp?franchise_id=23|archive-date=December 30, 2006|url-status=dead}} As of the end of the [[2023 NFL season|2023 season]], the Raiders have played 983 games in a total of 64 seasons in the AFL and NFL.{{cite web|url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/rai/|title=Oakland Raiders Franchise Encyclopedia|publisher=pro-football-reference.com|access-date=April 15, 2008|archive-date=July 7, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170707115845/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/rai/|url-status=live}} In those games, two coaches have won the [[Super Bowl]] with the team: [[John Madden]] in [[Super Bowl XI|1976]] and [[Tom Flores]] in [[Super Bowl XV|1980]] and [[Super Bowl XVIII|1983]]. One coach, [[John Rauch]] in [[AFL Championship#1967 Championship|1966]], won the [[AFL Championship]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/RaucJo0.htm |title=John Rauch |access-date=May 26, 2008 |publisher=pro-football-reference.com |archive-date=April 8, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190408161323/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/RaucJo0.htm |url-status=live }} Five other coaches, [[Art Shell]], [[Jack Del Rio]], [[Jon Gruden]], [[Bill Callahan (coach)|Bill Callahan]], and [[Rich Bisaccia]], have also taken the Raiders to the playoffs.{{cite web | publisher=NFLTeamHistory.com | url=http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/oakland_raiders/coaching_history.html | title=Oakland Raiders Coaching History | access-date=April 15, 2008 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080517084954/http://www.nflteamhistory.com/nfl_teams/oakland_raiders/coaching_history.html | archive-date=May 17, 2008 | url-status=dead }} Callahan led the Raiders to the Super Bowl. He did this in his first year as head coach of the team.{{cite web |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/CallBi0.htm |title=Bill Callahan |access-date=May 26, 2008 |publisher=pro-football-reference.com |archive-date=January 26, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130126201925/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/CallBi0.htm |url-status=live }} Shell and Gruden are the only coaches to have more than one tenure with the team, and Flores and Shell are the only coaches to have coached the team in both Oakland and Los Angeles. Gruden is the only coach to have coached the team in both Oakland and Las Vegas. Rauch is statistically the best, with a winning percentage of .805. However, the all-time leader in both games coached and wins is Madden, with 142 and 103 respectively. Of the 23 Raiders coaches, [[Al Davis]], Madden, and Flores are the only Raider coaches to be inducted into the [[Pro Football Hall of Fame]] for their contributions as coaches. Davis, who was also the Managing General partner and an AFL Commissioner, was in the Hall of Fame class of 1992, Madden was in the 2006 class, and Flores was in the 2021 class. Two coaches, Flores and Shell, are also former players for the Raiders. Shell was also inducted into the Hall of Fame in 1989 as a player.{{cite web | title=Pro Football Hall of Fame – Art Shell | publisher=Pro Football Hall of Fame |
